title: I Didn’t Want to Fly Fish, But I Loved Him Enough to Learn
description:
“Man with Fish” by Rebecca Swanberg “Rivers are roads which move, and which carry us whither we desire to go.” — Pascal We’d been driving in silence for some time. Nothing was wrong—nothing we could point to. It was early autumn. The larch trees had begun to yellow, and there was new snow in the […]

The post I Didn’t Want to Fly Fish, But I Loved Him Enough t
hook: She learned to fly fish because she loved him, and discovered a whole language of intimacy in rivers and silence.

title: Apache chef Nephi Craig says cooking Native food saved his life
description: Craig has cooked in a high-end restaurant — and for 7,800 prisoners in jail. He writes about cooking, his struggle with addition and his Native American heritage in the memoir Our Knives Will Save Us.
hook: A Native chef cooked for prisoners and billionaires alike. Both taught him that food is the only thing that doesn't lie.

title: The Ableism of Kings, the Fury of Trump
description:
I usually flinch from swashbuckling claims, but let me make this one at least: No hero from ancient myth feels as human as Odysseus. No one as relatable in their indomitable will to survive. Is it any wonder, then, that the Odyssey is having a moment, as humans seem barely to be surviving at all? […]

The post The Ableism of Kings, the Fury of Trump appeared first on
hook: Odysseus survives everything. In 2026, that makes him feel more human than any ancient hero has a right to.

title: The Red Mouth by Sheila Armstrong review – profound exploration of Ireland’s deep time
description:
Sinister bogland discoveries haunt the intersecting lives of four characters in this meditative, exquisitely written tale

Almost 14% of Ireland is bog: vast swathes of moss-carpeted land, below which layers of ancient history have been compounded into mulch-black turf. Captivated by their otherworldly beauty, Seamus Heaney wrote some of his finest poetry about bogs – and the bodies discovered, perfectly preserved, in their eerie depths.

Sheila Armstrong’s exquisite secon
hook: Irish bogs preserve ancient bodies and secrets. Armstrong's characters sink deeper into both with every page.

title: Reissue of the Week: Yoko Ono’s Season Of Glass
description:
Francis Buseko looks at the brilliant diaristic album made in the fade of tragedy which, while once mired in controversy now resembles the work of a coherent artistic vision

One of the tragedies of Yoko Ono’s career is that she has so often been treated as a symbol before being taken seriously as an artist in her own right. Season Of Glass arrived at
hook: Yoko Ono made a diaristic masterpiece in grief. The album once derided now looks like coherent art.
